images.caption: Figure 2:Theoretical Benchmarks.A.Compute time per positions relationship to model state size between backends across different sizes T.B.Compute time for forward and backwards pass between each backend.C.Peak Memory utilization between backends at T=128, K=4, C=3.D.Mapping the out of memory (OOM) frontier for each backend across different sizes of T.E.Comparing fused Triton kernel optimization to original linear scan and binary tree backends (pytorch-struct) for compute time per position across different sizes TF.Demonstrating Triton implementation maintains linear memory requirements across sizes T while Binary tree is able to be used as a viable compute backend.G.Demonstration of triton streaming backend performance at scales T=50,000, C=6, batch size=32 and variable size K.

Figure 3:Training convergence on TIMIT:K=1K{=}1linear CRF vs.K=30K{=}30semi-CRF.
Both models share an identical BiLSTM encoder; the only difference is the maximum
segment duration. The semi-CRF maintains a similar Phone Error Rate (PER) as the linear CRF, but consistent improvement at
boundary and segment F1 despite starting from a higher initial PER (larger state
space requires additional warmup).

Figure 4:Per-utterance posterior analysis on TIMIT utterance DR4_MPCS0_SI729
(T=480T=480frames).Top:Ground-truth (GT) phoneme segmentation.Middle panels:Position-marginal heatmapsp​(c∣t)p(c\mid t)for the
semi-CRF (K=30K{=}30, PER=0.232=0.232) and linear CRF (K=1K{=}1, PER=0.411=0.411).
Both models activate similar label hypotheses, but the semi-CRF concentrates
posterior mass more tightly on correct labels.Bottom:Boundary posteriorp​(boundary∣t)p(\mathrm{boundary}\mid t).
The semi-CRF (blue) varies across the utterance, peaking at true phoneme boundaries
(red dashed lines) and suppressing in segment interiors. The linear CRF (orange)
assignsp​(boundary∣t)=1.0p(\mathrm{boundary}\mid t)=1.0uniformly, the expected behavior when
every frame is its own segment (K=1K{=}1).
